one_line_takeaway: A visual-analytics system and LLM-assisted framework that lets practitioners evaluate LLM jailbreak success and reverse-engineer prompt design patterns via a novel 8-type prompt-component taxonomy, component perturbation, and keyword analysis.
key_finding: On assessment accuracy (400-item evaluation set), Llama 3.1 (405B) beat GPT-4o under default criteria (83.00% vs 77.50%); expert criteria refinement raised Llama 3.1 to 88.25% via a tabular/Excel baseline and to 92.25% via JailbreakLens, while roughly halving refinement time (123.33s vs 229.19s). For component classification (50 prompts, 841 sentences), GPT-4o reached 91.56% vs Llama's 88.47%. The case study on GPT-3.5 found >50% of jailbreak attacks succeeded (model vulnerable), that jailbreak success depends more on the TEMPLATE than the QUESTION, and that the Subject Characteristic component is the most impactful part of role-play prompts (deleting/switching it caused the largest performance drop); keywords like 'disregards'/'controversial' sat near Full Compliance while 'AIM'/'DAN' sat near Full Refusal (already defended).
core_contribution: JailbreakLens is a visual-analytics system plus an LLM-assisted analysis framework that helps model practitioners (red-teamers/defenders) evaluate LLM jailbreak performance and dissect jailbreak-prompt design patterns. Its own contributions are: (1) an LLM-based jailbreak-result assessment method that classifies model responses into a fine-grained 4-category result taxonomy (Full Refusal / Partial Refusal / Partial Compliance / Full Compliance), using 5-way self-consistency voting and user-refinable criteria; (2) a novel 8-type taxonomy of jailbreak-PROMPT COMPONENTS (Scene Introduction, Subject Characteristic, Task Operation, Output Specification, Output Constraint, Dialogue Example, Requirement Reminder, Question Placeholder) that the authors coded themselves, an LLM classifier that labels prompt sentences into these components, and three component-level perturbation strategies (delete, rephrase, switch) for what-if analysis; (3) a keyword-importance metric (TF-IDF x semantic similarity) with an importance-weighted performance score; and (4) the JailbreakLens interface (Configuration, Summary, Response, Keyword, Instance views). NOTE ON PRIOR WORK: the 4-category result taxonomy is adopted from GPTFuzzer [23], and the 10-class jailbreak-STRATEGY taxonomy plus the corpus (Character Role Play, Assumed Responsibility, etc.) are from Liu et al. [11]; these are cited inputs, not this paper's contribution. The paper's own new taxonomy is the component taxonomy.
threat_model: The paper is a defensive/analysis tool, not an attack. The threat it studies is jailbreak-prompt attacks: adversaries craft jailbreak TEMPLATES (e.g., role-play tricks like the 'Grandma Trick', 'DAN', 'AIM') that wrap prohibited questions to bypass an LLM's safety mechanisms and elicit disallowed content (illegal activities, harmful content, adult content, fraud, privacy violations per OpenAI's disallowed-usage list). Target users are model practitioners analyzing black-box models to find weaknesses and strengthen safety training and content moderation. The authors state they responsibly disclosed findings to OpenAI and frame the work as awareness/defense-oriented.
limitations: Model-agnostic/black-box only: it identifies WHICH components and keywords matter but does not explain the internal LLM mechanisms of jailbreak success (flagged as future work). The component taxonomy and annotation carry human subjectivity. Only 3 perturbation strategies (delete/rephrase/switch), not insert/crossover. Text-only; no multi-modal (MLLM) jailbreaks. The case study covers a single target model (GPT-3.5) with no cross-model comparison. Evaluation datasets are small, expert-built 'improvised' sets rather than a recognized benchmark, and assessment quantifies only compliance category, not response helpfulness/severity. As an HCI systems paper, effectiveness rests substantially on a single-expert case study and self-reported questionnaire scores.

datasets_used: Liu et al. [11] jailbreak-prompt corpus sourced from the JailbreakChat website (primary corpus; includes 10-class strategy taxonomy and prohibited-scenario questions)

Assessment evaluation dataset: 20 questions x 20 templates, 3 responses each from GPT-3.5 at temperature 2.0; 50 responses sampled per question, split 30 exploratory / 20 evaluation, yielding a 400-item evaluation set (manually labeled by experts)
Component-classification dataset: 50 randomly selected prompts comprising 841 prompt sentences
quantitative_results: Assessment accuracy, default criteria: GPT-4o 77.50%; Llama 3.1 (405B) 83.00%
Assessment accuracy, Llama 3.1 with refined criteria: Tabular/Excel UI 88.25% (avg 229.19s refinement); JailbreakLens 92.25% (avg 123.33s refinement)
GPT-4o per-component accuracy (Table IV diagonal): SI 0.92, SC 0.94, TO 0.82, OS 0.87, OC 0.87, DE 0.86, RR 0.96, QP 1.00
Case study (GPT-3.5): >50% of jailbreak attacks succeeded; 6 findings distilled

Expert questionnaire averages (1-5): evaluate jailbreak performance 4.83, analyze components 4.50, explore keywords 4.67, refine instances 4.67, easy to learn 4.17, easy to use 4.83, will reuse 4.83, design understandable 4.67, not overwhelming 4.50
baselines_compared: Assessment models: GPT-4o vs Llama 3.1 (405B) under default criteria
Criteria-refinement interface: tabular UI (Microsoft Excel) vs JailbreakLens (measured on accuracy and refinement time)
Keyword view: chosen coordinate-space design vs an alternative four-word-cloud design (qualitative expert preference)
